title: Mujadara
subtitle: Lentils and rice with caramelised onions
excerpt: Green lentils cooked with rice and cumin, topped with onions fried until deeply caramelised and almost crisp. One of the great simple dishes of the Levant — cheap, filling, and complex in flavour from very few ingredients. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Mujadara is one of the oldest recorded dishes in the Arab world, appearing across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Jordan, and Egypt. Documented by Claudia Roden in A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968) and by Anissa Helou in Feast: Food of the Islamic World (2018). The caramelised onion topping is the defining element — the longer they cook, the better.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps refrigerated for four days. Reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The caramelised onions can be made two days ahead and kept refrigerated. Reheat in a pan before serving.

content.text: The caramelised onions are the heart of mujadara. They need a full 30 minutes over medium heat, stirring regularly, until they are deep brown, sweet, and almost crisp at the edges. This is not a step that can be hurried — pale, soft onions on top of the lentils produce a flat, bland dish. Properly caramelised onions make everything underneath taste richer.

items.prepNote: thinly sliced — half for the base, half for the topping
divided — more for the topping onions

content.text: Heat half the olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add 2 sliced onions and cook for 10 minutes until soft and golden. Add {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin and {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon; stir for 1 minute. Add {{lentils-green}} of rinsed lentils and cover with 900 ml of water. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, cover, and simmer for 15 minutes. Add {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and stir to combine. If the water looks low, add a splash. Cover and cook for 18–20 minutes until the rice is tender and the liquid absorbed. Remove from the heat and leave covered for 5 minutes.

While the lentils cook, heat the remaining olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the remaining 2 sliced onions with a pinch of salt. Cook for 25–30 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until the onions are deep brown and starting to crisp at the edges. They will reduce to about a quarter of their original volume. Drain briefly on kitchen paper.
Pile the lentil and rice mixture onto a serving plate or serve from the pan. Top generously with the caramelised onions. Serve with yoghurt, a simple salad of cucumber and tomato, and lemon wedges.

items.symptom: Rice and lentils sticking to the bottom
items.cause: Heat too high or not enough water during the rice cooking stage.
items.fix: Once the rice is added, keep the heat at the lowest setting. The crust on the bottom (called hkaka in Arabic) is actually considered the best part — but only if it's golden, not burnt.

items.cause: Heat too low, or pan too crowded so onions steam rather than fry.
items.fix: Use a wide pan. Medium heat is the key — too low and they steam, too high and they burn before caramelising. Patience and regular stirring over 30 minutes is the only method.

items.cause: Not enough water or not enough time before the rice went in.
items.fix: The lentils should be nearly done before the rice is added. If they still feel chalky at 15 minutes, give them 5 more minutes and add a splash of water.

content.text: Mujadara is one of the oldest dishes in the Arab culinary tradition, documented as far back as the 13th century. The name means pockmarked in Arabic, a reference to the lentils dotted through the rice. It is the everyday food of Lebanon, Syria, and Palestine — made when the fridge is nearly empty, eaten as a main course with yoghurt and raw vegetables, and considered by many people to be one of the most satisfying things you can cook. The combination of lentils, rice, and caramelised onions creates a complete protein and a depth of flavour that punches above what three ingredients should be able to achieve.
